title: The Carbon Market Explorer
description: How is carbon priced, traded, and sold? Explore the voluntary and compliance markets — and the controversies that surround them.

stages.title: Can You Put a Price on the Planet?
stages.body:
Carbon markets are supposed to be simple: **make polluters pay, fund those who remove carbon.** In practice, they're one of the most complex and controversial tools in the climate fight.

Two markets exist:
1. **Compliance markets** (like the EU ETS) — legally mandated, covering ~23% of global emissions
2. **Voluntary markets** — companies buy credits to offset their footprint

In 2025, the **voluntary market was worth $2.2 billion** and the **EU ETS alone hit $909 billion**.

But 30-40% of voluntary credits may not represent real reductions. The system is broken — but is the concept itself flawed?

stages.title: Markets Compared
stages.description: How do the two major carbon market types actually differ?

stages.title: EU Carbon Price History
stages.description: Trace the EU ETS price from launch to today — it tells the story of climate policy ambition.

questions.question: What was the EU ETS carbon price at its 2023 peak?
questions.explanation: The EU ETS price hit €100 in February 2023, a historic milestone.

questions.question: Which is larger: the compliance or voluntary carbon market?
questions.explanation: The compliance market ($909B) is ~400x larger than the voluntary market ($2.2B).

questions.question: What percentage of voluntary credits may not represent real reductions?
questions.explanation: Studies suggest 30-40% of voluntary carbon credits don't deliver promised reductions.

questions.question: Why did carbon prices crash in 2012?
questions.explanation: A massive oversupply of free allowances caused the EU ETS price to collapse to under €5.

stages.title: Markets Are Tools — Not Solutions
stages.body:
Carbon markets are powerful but imperfect. The price signal matters — **$100/ton changes behavior**. But markets alone won't solve this crisis.

Real solutions require **systemic transformation**: switching energy systems, protecting forests, redesigning cities, and rethinking how we produce and consume.

Let's explore what those solutions actually look like.
